---
title: "Dataset catalog"
engine: julia
julia:
  exeflags: ["--project", "--threads=auto"]
---

::: {.objectives}
A reference for the datasets that recur throughout these materials. For each one you'll find
what it is, where it came from, how to load it, and which pages use it. Skim it now to get a
feel for the recurring examples, and come back whenever you need the details for a particular
analysis.
:::

# How datasets are loaded

All example datasets are loaded through a single function re-exported by the course package:

```{julia}
#| output: false
using DataFrames
using SMLP2026: dataset
```

```{julia}
sleepstudy = DataFrame(dataset(:sleepstudy))
first(sleepstudy, 5)
```

`dataset(name)` resolves a name from one of two sources:

1. **Datasets re-exported from [MixedModelsDatasets.jl](https://juliamixedmodels.github.io/MixedModelsDatasets.jl/stable/datasets/)** — the standard collection bundled with the Julia mixed-models ecosystem (e.g. `sleepstudy`, `kb07`, `mrk17_exp1`).
2. **Course-specific datasets hosted on [OSF](https://osf.io/)** — downloaded on first use, checksum-verified, and cached locally (e.g. `fggk21`, `kwdyz11`, `kkl15`). The download is automatic; you don't need to fetch anything by hand.

To list everything available in this project:

```{julia}
using SMLP2026
sort(SMLP2026.datasets())
```

::: {.callout-note}
A few datasets in these materials are also distributed as files under `data/` (e.g. the raw
Emotikon data as `emotikon.rds`). Those are used in the data-wrangling example
[Saving data with Arrow](../reference/arrow.qmd); for everyday analysis prefer
`dataset(...)`.
:::

# Datasets bundled with MixedModelsDatasets.jl

These three are documented in full upstream — design, every column, and the primary
citation — in the [MixedModelsDatasets.jl dataset reference](https://juliamixedmodels.github.io/MixedModelsDatasets.jl/stable/datasets/).
We summarize their role in this course and link out for the details.

## `sleepstudy`

Reaction times from a sleep-deprivation study: average response time per day for 18 subjects
restricted to 3 hours in bed per night. The canonical first example for a linear mixed model
with a random slope for time. See the [upstream entry](https://juliamixedmodels.github.io/MixedModelsDatasets.jl/stable/datasets/#sleepstudy).

**Used in:** [sleepstudy](../lmm-intro/sleepstudy.qmd), [singularity](../lmm-intro/singularity.qmd), [transformations](../contrasts/transformation.qmd), [speed metrics](../contrasts/sleepstudy_speed.qmd), [bootstrap](../inference/bootstrap.qmd), [GLMM](../glmm/glmm.qmd).

## `kb07`

Response times from Kronmüller & Barr (2007), a 2×2×2 visual-world experiment widely used to
illustrate maximal versus parsimonious random-effects structures. See the
[upstream entry](https://juliamixedmodels.github.io/MixedModelsDatasets.jl/stable/datasets/#kb07).

**Used in:** [shrinkage plots](../visualization/shrinkageplot.qmd), [bootstrap](../inference/bootstrap.qmd), [profiling](../inference/profiling.qmd), [kb07](../inference/kb07.qmd), [power simulation](../inference/power_simulation.qmd).

## `mrk17_exp1`

Lexical-decision response times from Masson, Rabe & Kliegl (2017), Experiment 1 — a 2×2×2
design that is a standard benchmark for high-dimensional crossed random effects. The course
refers to it as **`mrk17`**. See the
[upstream entry](https://juliamixedmodels.github.io/MixedModelsDatasets.jl/stable/datasets/#mrk17_exp1).

**Used in:** [model specification](../model-specification/mrk17.qmd).

# Course-specific datasets

These datasets are hosted on OSF and downloaded automatically by `dataset(...)`. Column
summaries below are generated directly from the data, so they stay accurate as the datasets
are updated.

## `fggk21` (the Emotikon project)

**Full name:** Physical fitness of third-graders (Emotikon study)

Physical-fitness component scores from @Fuehner2021: 108,295 third-graders from 515 primary
schools across 9 cohorts, tested on a battery of fitness tasks (running, jumping, balancing,
and so on). The size and nested structure (children within schools within cohorts) make it a
realistic large-scale example for contrast coding of a multi-level factor (`Test`), for
PCA-guided model complexification, and for visualization at scale.

**Citation:** @Fuehner2021.

```{julia}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "columns of fggk21"
describe(DataFrame(dataset(:fggk21)))
```

**Used in:** [contrast coding](../contrasts/contrasts_fggk21.qmd), [AoG plots](../visualization/AoGPlots.qmd), [Emotikon capstone](../examples/emotikon/fggk21.qmd), [comparing transformed metrics](../examples/emotikon/check_emotikon_transform.qmd).

::: {.callout-note title="Related files"}
Pre-aggregated variants `fggk21_Child` and `fggk21_Score` are also available via
`dataset(...)`, and the raw `emotikon.rds` lives under `data/`. The data-wrangling steps that
produce the Arrow files are walked through in [Saving data with Arrow](../reference/arrow.qmd).
:::

## `kwdyz11`

**Full name:** Kliegl, Wei, Dambacher, Yan & Zhou (2011) visual-attention experiment

Reaction times from a cueing experiment with four cue-target relations (CTRs), used to
estimate spatial, object-based, and attractor-like effects of visual attention together with
the individual differences in those effects. A key teaching point: the attraction effect is
near zero as a fixed effect but has a reliable variance component, and the originally reported
model turned out to be singular — motivating the replication in `kkl15`.

**Citation:** @Kliegl2011.

```{julia}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "columns of kwdyz11"
describe(DataFrame(dataset(:kwdyz11)))
```

**Used in:** [contrast coding of visual-attention effects](../contrasts/contrasts_kwdyz11.qmd), [visual-attention capstone](../examples/visual-attention/kwdyz11.qmd).

## `kkl15`

**Full name:** Kliegl, Kushela & Laubrock (2015) replication and extension

A larger conceptual replication of `kwdyz11` [@Kliegl2015], run at Potsdam with additional
manipulations of target size and rectangle orientation. With more data and more conditions it
estimates the theoretically critical spatial–attraction correlation parameter that was the
source of the singularity in the original study, and is the running example for principled
reduction of model complexity.

**Citation:** @Kliegl2015.

```{julia}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "columns of kkl15"
describe(DataFrame(dataset(:kkl15)))
```

**Used in:** [replication & model reduction](../examples/visual-attention/kkl15.qmd), and as the larger companion to [`kwdyz11`](../examples/visual-attention/kwdyz11.qmd).

---

*This page was rendered from git revision {{< git-rev short=true >}} using Quarto {{< version >}} and Julia {{< julia-version >}}.*
